bfbtools 0.1.0
  • Reference
  • Articles
    • The bfbtools Algorithms

    Find the nearest BFB-admitting count-vector (noisy/weighted search)

    nearest_bfb.Rd

    Finds the highest-weight BFB count-vector explaining an observed, potentially noisy count-vector, using the linear-fold-core weighted search (ported from Zakov, Kinsella & Bafna 2013's algorithm family; see src/distance.cpp for the specific design). Unlike an L1-ball neighbor search bounded by a fixed radius (which can miss the true nearest vector or require tuning the radius – the approach an earlier, exponential-search-based implementation of this package used), this performs an unbounded, Pareto-frontier-pruned search under an explicit probabilistic error model, and finds the *exact* highest-weight vector within the search's weight cutoff (not an approximation).

    nearest_bfb(
  n_vec,
  model = c("poisson", "none"),
  min_weight = 1e-06,
  max_frontier = 2e+05
)

    Arguments

    n_vec

    observed integer count-vector, telomere to centromere.

    model

    error model: "poisson" (default; observed counts modeled as noisy Poisson draws around the true count) or "none" (no error tolerated – equivalent to admits_bfb, provided for consistency/testing).

    min_weight

    search cutoff: candidates whose cumulative weight would fall below this value are pruned (default 1e-6). Lower values search further from the observed vector at the cost of more work.

    max_frontier

    safety cap on the number of live candidates tracked at once; returns NA (with a warning) rather than growing unboundedly if this is hit (default 200000).

    Value

    a list with weight (in (0, 1], 1 = exact match) and vector (the nearest/corrected BFB count-vector) – or NULL (with a warning) if nothing was found within min_weight, or NA if max_frontier was hit.

    Examples

    nearest_bfb(c(14, 7, 18, 16, 9, 12))  # paper's Fig. 5 near-miss example
#> $weight
#> [1] 0.9114639
#> 
#> $vector
#> [1] 14  7 19 17  9 13
#>

    Contents
